full_response:
We consider the outgoing radial null geodesic that starts at the origin (r = 0) when the flashlight is switched on at coordinate time t = 0.  
For such a geodesic we were given

t(r) = ℓ arctan ( r / ℓ ).     (1)

1. Time needed to reach r → ∞  

As r → ∞, arctan(r/ℓ) → π/2, hence

t_∞ = ℓ (π/2) = (πℓ)/2.     (2)

2. Radial position at the later time t = 2 t_∞  

First invert (1):

r(t) = ℓ tan ( t / ℓ ).     (3)

Now set t = 2 t_∞ = πℓ:

r(2 t_∞) = ℓ tan ( (πℓ) / ℓ ) = ℓ tan(π) = ℓ · 0 = 0.     (4)

Thus the light ray, after reaching spatial infinity in the AdS–like spacetime, returns to the origin in another interval of the same coordinate time.

\boxed{t_\infty = \dfrac{\pi\,\ell}{2}}, \qquad \boxed{r(2t_\infty)=0}

Confidence: 82%
